title: Motor Control Methods: H-Bridge · Motor Driver IC · Transistor

zones.label: H-Bridge Circuit
zones.summary: Four switches arranged in an H — enables full forward and reverse motor control
facts: Named 'H-bridge' because the circuit diagram looks like the letter H
Four switches (S1–S4) connect motor terminals to power and ground
Forward: close S1 (top-left) + S4 (bottom-right); current flows left to right through motor
Reverse: close S2 (bottom-left) + S3 (top-right); current flows right to left through motor
Coast (free-wheel): open all four switches — motor spins down on its own
Brake: close both bottom switches (S2+S4) — short-circuits motor terminals to GND, stops quickly
Shoot-through danger: NEVER close S1+S2 or S3+S4 simultaneously — this short-circuits the power supply directly to ground
In real circuits, BJT or MOSFET transistors replace the mechanical switches
A DPDT (double-pole double-throw) mechanical switch implements a manual H-bridge
Motor driver ICs implement this concept in silicon with built-in protection
Concept taught in Chapter 6; foundational to understanding all DC motor direction control

zones.label: Motor Driver IC
zones.summary: Integrated chip with two H-bridges — the practical choice for all course robot projects
facts: An IC that contains two complete H-bridge circuits in one package
On the Cytron Maker Pi RP2040: MX1508 dual H-bridge driver IC
MX1508 specs: 1 A continuous, 1.5 A peak (5 s) per channel; motor supply 2–10 V
Common alternative: TB6612FNG (1.2 A continuous, 3.2 A peak; 2.7–13.5 V VM)
Control pins per channel: IN1, IN2 (direction) + PWM (speed from MCU)
IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW → forward; IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH → reverse; both LOW → brake
PWM duty cycle sets speed: 0% = stopped, 100% = full speed
Built-in flyback diodes absorb back-EMF spikes when motor stops or reverses — protects MCU
Built-in dead-time logic prevents shoot-through — safe to use without timing worries
MicroPython on Cytron board: two GPIO pairs (M1A/M1B, M2A/M2B) + two PWM pins
Best for: all robot projects in this course — safe, simple, two-channel integrated solution

zones.label: Single Transistor
zones.summary: One transistor controls on/off or speed via PWM — no direction reversal
facts: A single NPN transistor (e.g., 2N2222 for small motors, TIP120 for larger) acts as an electronic switch
Base (B): connected to MCU GPIO via a current-limiting resistor (~1 kΩ for 2N2222)
Collector (C): connected to the motor's negative terminal
Emitter (E): connected to GND
Motor positive terminal: connected to the power supply (3–12 V depending on motor)
GPIO HIGH → transistor ON → current flows through motor → motor spins
GPIO LOW → transistor OFF → no current → motor stops (or coasts)
PWM on the GPIO pin controls motor speed by rapidly switching the transistor on and off
Limitation: current always flows the same direction — motor cannot be reversed
A flyback diode MUST be added across motor terminals to absorb back-EMF spikes
To add direction control you need four transistors (full H-bridge) or a motor driver IC
Best for: simple on/off loads (fan, pump, one-direction conveyor, beginner experiments)

quiz.question: Which motor control method uses four switches in an H shape to allow both forward and reverse motor direction?
quiz.explanation: The H-bridge gets its name from the H-shaped circuit of four switches. Closing the diagonal pair S1+S4 drives the motor forward; closing the opposite diagonal S2+S3 reverses it. All motor driver ICs implement this concept inside their silicon.

quiz.question: Which motor control IC is built into the Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 board used in this course?
quiz.explanation: The Cytron Maker Pi RP2040 uses the MX1508 dual H-bridge driver IC — rated at 1 A continuous and 1.5 A peak per channel. It handles two DC motors, each controlled by a direction pair (IN1/IN2) plus a PWM speed pin.

quiz.question: Which method can control motor SPEED with PWM but cannot reverse the motor's direction?
quiz.explanation: A single NPN transistor switches current on and off. A PWM signal on the base varies the duty cycle and thus the average current through the motor — controlling speed. But current always flows the same direction through the motor, so it cannot reverse without additional circuitry.

quiz.question: Which method risks a short circuit (shoot-through) if switches on the same leg are closed at the same time?
quiz.explanation: In an H-bridge, closing S1+S2 (or S3+S4) simultaneously connects the power supply directly to ground — called shoot-through. This destroys transistors and can damage the power supply. Motor driver ICs have built-in dead-time logic to prevent this.

quiz.question: Which method requires adding an external flyback diode across the motor terminals to prevent damage from back-EMF spikes?
quiz.explanation: When a transistor switches off, the motor's collapsing magnetic field generates a voltage spike (back-EMF) that can exceed the transistor's breakdown voltage. A flyback diode in parallel with the motor clamps this spike. Motor driver ICs have flyback diodes built in, so external ones are not needed.
